nodes.label: Algernon Blackwood
nodes.note: Nature as hostile cosmic presence; a core touchstone.

nodes.label: Arthur Machen
nodes.note: Occult-industrial dread; uncanny that steps into the room.

nodes.label: Robert Aickman
nodes.note: Strange stories; dread that outlasts the reading.

nodes.label: Stefan Grabiński
nodes.note: Polish weird fiction; motion and machinery haunted.

nodes.label: Shirley Jackson
works: We Have Always Lived in the Castle

nodes.label: Fritz Leiber
nodes.note: Urban supernatural; Smoke Ghost is a touchstone.

nodes.label: Lisa Tuttle
nodes.note: Dread out of view, then a real monster.

nodes.label: Dorothy B. Hughes
nodes.note: The villain as protagonist; interior inescapability.

nodes.label: Agatha Christie
nodes.note: Proto-slasher structure; the group picked off.

nodes.label: W.G. Sebald
nodes.note: Accretive essay-novels; the past contaminating the present.

nodes.label: Karl Ove Knausgård
nodes.note: The uncanny bleeding into ordinary life.

nodes.label: Benjamín Labatut
works: When We Cease to Understand the World

nodes.label: Joe R. Lansdale
nodes.note: Serious horror and drama only — not the humor.

nodes.label: Stephen Graham Jones
nodes.note: Selective: relentless-antagonist work, not the genre-referential.

nodes.label: Stephen King
nodes.note: Survival compressed into a single trap.

nodes.label: Marguerite Duras
nodes.note: Dreamlike register; dread without a tonal seam.

nodes.label: Juan Rulfo
nodes.note: Dead-town ghost story; compression and method.

nodes.label: Daphne du Maurier
nodes.note: Dreamlike shifting suddenly into the concrete.

nodes.label: Rick Bass
nodes.note: Not horror; awe from basic expressive elements.

nodes.label: Mariana Enriquez
works: Things We Lost in the Fire

nodes.label: Demián Rugna
nodes.note: Argentine horror; dread plus concrete threat.

nodes.label: Valancourt Books
nodes.note: Recovered Gothic and weird fiction; reliable fit.

nodes.label: Word Horde
nodes.note: Contemporary weird in the Barron lineage.

nodes.label: Leo Perutz
works: By Night Under the Stone Bridge

nodes.label: M.R. James
nodes.note: Suggestion, then the thing is in the room.
works: Oh, Whistle, and I'll Come to You

nodes.label: E.T.A. Hoffmann
nodes.note: Source of the Central European uncanny.

nodes.label: Laird Barron
nodes.note: Cosmic dread materializing; skip The Croning, read the stories.

nodes.label: Peter Straub
nodes.note: Atmospheric presence that gains weight and agency.

nodes.label: Thomas Tryon
nodes.note: American Gothic; folk horror discovered too late.

nodes.label: Ramsey Campbell
nodes.note: Hit or miss across a huge output; strong at his best.

nodes.label: John Farris
works: All Heads Turn When the Hunt Goes By

nodes.label: Derek Raymond
nodes.note: Almost horror in register; no procedural comfort.
works: He Died With His Eyes Open

nodes.label: David Goodis
nodes.note: Protagonists already destroyed before page one.

nodes.label: Cornell Woolrich
nodes.note: Paranoid urban dread; supernatural treated straight.

nodes.label: Léo Malet
nodes.note: Founding French noir; Série Noire lineage.

nodes.label: John le Carré
nodes.note: Institutional betrayal; the plot larger than the protagonist.
works: The Spy Who Came in From the Cold

nodes.label: Simon Urban
nodes.note: Alternate-history noir; conspiracy outside the frame.

nodes.label: Leonardo Sciascia
nodes.note: De Maria's closest neighbor; conspiracy unacknowledged.

nodes.label: Stanisław Lem
nodes.note: The universe declines to explain itself.

nodes.label: Danilo Kiš
nodes.note: The historical record as active threat.

nodes.label: Muriel Spark
nodes.note: The ending telegraphed; pure structural dread.

nodes.label: Paul Auster
nodes.note: The investigator dissolves into the investigation.

nodes.label: Ismail Kadare
nodes.note: Bureaucratic supernatural; the state as antagonist.

nodes.label: Friedrich Dürrenmatt
nodes.note: The detective story that refuses to resolve.

nodes.label: Dino Buzzati
nodes.note: The dread is the waiting itself.

nodes.label: Emmanuel Carrère
nodes.note: Documentary structure meeting what it can't account for.

nodes.label: Patrick Modiano
nodes.note: Investigations of the past that won't resolve.

nodes.label: David Morrell
nodes.note: Stripped to survival; bleaker than the film.

nodes.label: Guy N. Smith
nodes.note: Pulpy but the threat is concrete.

nodes.label: John Hawkes
nodes.note: The most Lynch-like novelist; menace structural.

nodes.label: Denis Johnson
nodes.note: Violence arrives mid-sentence, no warning shift.

nodes.label: Carlos Fuentes
nodes.note: The ending folds back on itself.

nodes.label: Jerzy Kosiński
nodes.note: Scenes significant in ways never confirmed.

nodes.label: Boris Vian
nodes.note: Whimsical and associative, turning genuinely dark.

nodes.label: Georges Perec
nodes.note: Playful structure turning into real horror.
works: W, or the Memory of Childhood

nodes.label: Kelly Link
nodes.note: Off-kilter and funny; then something wrong arrives.

nodes.label: Patricia Lockwood
nodes.note: Associative and funny; hard pivot, same voice.
works: No One Is Talking About This

nodes.label: Kevin Barry
nodes.note: Digressive Irish naturalism; darkness without a seam.

nodes.label: Tarjei Vesaas
nodes.note: Awe and dread in landscape; Hamsun's heir.

nodes.label: Larry Brown
nodes.note: Menace in the ordinary; bare Southern prose.

nodes.label: Juan Pablo Villalobos
nodes.note: Horror in the gap of a child narrator.

nodes.label: Bora Chung
nodes.note: Folk and body horror taken straight.

nodes.label: Jung Yu-jin
nodes.note: Cold narrator; horror in the Highsmith sense.

nodes.label: Ben Okri
nodes.note: Spirit world bleeding into the material.

nodes.label: William Golding
nodes.note: Consciousness and body failing; no comfort.

nodes.label: Flannery O'Connor
nodes.note: Spiritual and physical grotesque at once.
works: A Good Man Is Hard to Find

nodes.label: Hilary Mantel
nodes.note: Folk horror in a village; genuinely strange.

nodes.label: Alan Garner
nodes.note: Myth as trap embedded in landscape.

nodes.label: David Lindsay
nodes.note: Cosmic horror as fantasy; bleak vision.

nodes.label: Gene Wolfe
works: The Book of the New Sun

nodes.label: R. Scott Bakker
nodes.note: Epic fantasy in a horror register; Ligotti-adjacent.

nodes.label: Edgar Allan Poe
nodes.note: The root of American horror interiority.

nodes.label: Nathaniel Hawthorne
works: The House of the Seven Gables

nodes.label: Ueda Akinari
nodes.note: Edo-period ghost tales; elegant and cold.

nodes.label: Lafcadio Hearn
nodes.note: Japanese ghost folklore, collected and retold.

nodes.label: Guy de Maupassant
nodes.note: Psychological dissolution; The Horla is the peak.

nodes.label: Jeremias Gotthelf
nodes.note: The Black Spider: village sin as contagion.

nodes.label: Clive Barker
nodes.note: Books of Blood: visceral imagination, no camp.

nodes.label: Gabino Iglesias
nodes.note: The Devil Takes You Home yes; others less so.

nodes.label: Yōko Ogawa
nodes.note: Quiet systemic dread; The Memory Police.

nodes.label: William W. Johnstone
nodes.note: Pulpy but the threat is relentless.

nodes.label: Fred Vargas
nodes.note: The supernatural hovers at the edges.

nodes.label: Koji Suzuki
nodes.note: Philosophical dread under the famous premise.

nodes.label: Ursula K. Le Guin
works: The Word for World Is Forest

nodes.label: Sheridan Le Fanu
nodes.note: The Victorian master of oblique haunting.

nodes.label: Henry James
nodes.note: Ambiguity as the engine of dread.

nodes.label: Edith Wharton
works: The Ghost Stories of Edith Wharton

nodes.label: Robert W. Chambers
nodes.note: The King in Yellow: contagious text.

nodes.label: Lord Dunsany
nodes.note: Dream-geography; the root of weird fantasy.

nodes.label: Robert E. Howard
nodes.note: Violent weird adventure; Pigeons From Hell.

nodes.label: Oliver Onions
nodes.note: The Beckoning Fair One: slow possession.

nodes.label: W.F. Harvey
nodes.note: August Heat and The Beast with Five Fingers.

nodes.label: Pu Songling
nodes.note: Strange tales; fox spirits and scholars.
works: Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio

nodes.label: Izumi Kyōka
works: The Holy Man of Mount Koya

nodes.label: Fyodor Sologub
nodes.note: The Petty Demon: paranoia as weather.

nodes.label: Marcel Schwob
works: The King in the Golden Mask

nodes.label: Charles Beaumont
nodes.note: Twilight Zone-era unease, sharper in print.

nodes.label: Anne Rivers Siddons
nodes.note: The House Next Door: suburban wrongness.

nodes.label: Harlan Ellison
works: I Have No Mouth and I Must Scream

nodes.label: Joyce Carol Oates
works: Where Are You Going, Where Have You Been?

nodes.label: Roland Topor
nodes.note: The Tenant: identity dissolving in an apartment.

nodes.label: Adam Nevill
nodes.note: British folk and apartment horror, committed.

nodes.label: John Langan
nodes.note: The Fisherman: grief opening onto the cosmic.

nodes.label: Michael Cisco
nodes.note: The Divinity Student: pure oneiric weird.

nodes.label: Catriona Ward
works: The Last House on Needless Street

nodes.label: Olga Tokarczuk
nodes.note: Drive Your Plow: fate, astrology, bodies.
works: Drive Your Plow Over the Bones of the Dead

nodes.label: Mia Couto
nodes.note: Mozambican dream-realism with war under it.

nodes.label: China Miéville
nodes.note: The weird city; dread in the architecture.

nodes.label: Julio Cortázar
nodes.note: House Taken Over; the uncanny domestic.

nodes.label: Jorge Luis Borges
nodes.note: Infinite libraries; dread of the infinite.
